name: Oswald Wirth Tarot — The 22 Esoteric Arcana (1889 / 1926)
description:
# Oswald Wirth Tarot

## At a glance

The **first tarot conceived as an explicitly occult, initiatic instrument** (distinct from Etteilla's earlier *divination* deck of 1788) — 22 Major Arcana only, designed in **1889** by
**Oswald Wirth** under the direction of the Kabbalist **Stanislas de Guaita**, realising
the program of **Éliphas Lévi**. It fixes the marriage of tarot trumps to the **Hebrew
alphabet** and the Kabbalah that defines Continental (and later English) esoteric tarot.

## Origin & dating

Wirth, immersed in the French Occult Revival, was commissioned by de Guaita to draw 22
trumps "restored to hieroglyphic purity" per Lévi's ideas. Published **1889** as *Les 22
Arcanes du Tarot Kabbalistique* (hand-colored, gilt-edged like a grimoire). Wirth issued
a revised, wider-known version in **1926**. It reinterprets the **Marseille** trumps
through Kabbalah, alchemy, Hermeticism and Masonic/Rosicrucian symbolism.

## Provenance & evidence

The 1889 and 1926 decks and Wirth's accompanying writings survive. The lineage is
explicit and documented: **Lévi → de Guaita → Wirth**, the French esoteric current that
Lévi launched in the 1850s by asserting a tarot-Kabbalah correspondence.

## Game or divination?

**Esoteric/divinatory by design** — never a game deck. Note it is **trumps only** (22
cards): a philosophical/meditative object, the Continental sibling of what the Golden
Dawn was doing in England.

## The fear question

**Openly published, but inside a subculture.** Wirth and de Guaita were public figures of
a fashionable Parisian occult *movement* (salons, orders, presses). There was no legal
peril for these bourgeois esotericists; the "secrecy" was the deliberate mystique of
initiatory orders, not fear of prosecution. Contrast again the jobbing fortune-teller:
respectable occult *philosophy* was safe; commercial *fortune-telling* was the
criminalised, déclassé version of the same impulse.

## Counter-voices

Wirth's deck embodies the esoteric tradition's own self-understanding: that it is
*restoring* ancient symbolic truth (Lévi's Kabbalah-tarot link). The historical caveat:
Lévi's Hebrew-letter assignment was an **invention of the 1850s**, not a medieval
survival. But unlike the Egyptian myth, the Wirth deck is honest about being a
*construction* — it openly builds a new symbolic system on the Marseille, which is
exactly what the grammar should record (a deliberate esoteric overlay, well documented,
not a false antiquity claim).

## Open questions / corrections owed

- Distinguish the 1889 and 1926 versions in the grammar (de Guaita-directed vs. Wirth's
  mature revision).
- Wirth's Hebrew-letter/path assignments differ from the Golden Dawn's — do **not**
  conflate the two correspondence systems.

## Sources & further reading

- "Oswald Wirth Tarot," Wikipedia; "The Oswald Wirth Tarot," designdcl / tarotator.
- Stanislas de Guaita and Éliphas Lévi primary materials (public domain).

sections.Symbolism: A wanderer in motley strides forward, a bundle on a staff, while a creature tears at his leg and a crocodile waits ahead. Wirth places the Fool between Judgement and the World — the unnumbered force of irrational, ungovernable spirit that animates the whole sequence.

**Hebrew letter:** ש (Shin, 'tooth, fire') — the unnumbered letter in Eliphas Levi's attribution that Wirth follows.

**Number:** 0.

**Astrological association:** the element of Fire; the free spirit unbound by the zodiac.
sections.Upright: Freedom, the impulse to move without calculation, spirit that cannot be domesticated. The energy that begins and ends the cycle, prior to all numbering.
sections.Reversed: Aimlessness, madness, dispersion of force; the spirit that scatters instead of advancing.
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** Keeps the Marseille *Mat* (unnumbered vagabond) and assigns **ש Shin ("tooth, fire")** — Lévi was **the first to link the Fool with Shin**, and Wirth follows him [@web_levi_arrangement; @hermetics_marathakis]. **Critical placement point:** in **Lévi's and Wirth's scheme the Fool is unnumbered and sits between Trump XX (Judgement) and Trump XXI (The World)** — it is *not* "card 0" at the head of the deck. That Fool-first-as-0 = Aleph arrangement is the **Golden Dawn's** different system. *(The grammar lists Le Fou at sort_order 0 with Shin; the letter is right but the position implies the Golden Dawn order — flag.)* [@web_levi_arrangement; @web_gd_swap].

sections.Wirth:
[Translated from Wirth's *Le Tarot des imagiers du Moyen Âge*, 1927]

The Fool — zero, unnumbered, the card that stands outside the sequence and within every part of it — shows a wandering figure in particoloured jester's costume, a bundle on his back, a dog (or crocodile, in some versions) biting at his heels as he steps cheerfully toward the edge of a precipice. He does not look down.

The Fool's zero is not nothing: it is the circle, the point of pure potentiality before number begins, and also the point to which all numbers return. He is simultaneously the beginning (before Arc. I) and the end (after Arc. XXI): the divine child setting out on the adventure of individuation, and the regenerated initiate returning to innocence on the far side of experience.

Wirth insists on the ambivalence of the card: the Fool is both the unawakened soul who wanders in unconsciousness, and the sage who has gone beyond conventional categories and thus appears foolish to those still bound by them. The bite of the dog (or crocodile) at his heels is the bite of the material world trying to hold him back — he is either too unaware to notice it, or too free to be detained by it.

His bundle contains everything he has accumulated through the cycle — but he carries it lightly, behind him, as an afterthought rather than a burden. This is the lightness of the one who possesses without being possessed, who has been enriched by experience without being imprisoned by it. The Fool's path is the path of eternal beginning.

1st Hebrew letter (Aleph).

ORIGIN OF THE SYMBOLISM OF THE FIRST CARD OF THE TAROT.

The Aleph hieroglyphically expresses Man himself as a
collective unity, the master principle, ruler of the earth.

From this hieroglyphic meaning are derived ideas of
the Unity and of the principle which détermines it, ideas
which give to Aleph its value as the sign of Power and
Stability.

Man, or the Microcosm, the Unity and the Principle in
all the worlds, is the meaning of the primitive hieroglyphic,
which, as we see, exactly renders the general ideas which
we have established.

But attentive consideration of this first card of the
Tarot will enlighten us still further.

Symbolism of the First Card of the Tarot.
THE JUGGLER.

If you take the first card of the Tarot and examine it
attentively, you will see that the form of the juggler
depicted upon it corresponds in all points with that of the
letter Aleph. If we now apply to the study of this card
the principles of the elucidation of symbolism, according
to the Traité Élémentaire de Science Occulte, we at once find
new explanations of it.

The top of the figure is occupied by the divine sign of
Universal Life oo placed upon the head of the Juggler.

The bottom of the figure represents the Earth orna-
mented with its productions, the symbol of Nature.

Lastly, the centre is occupied by the Man himself, placed
behind a table covered with divers objects.

The right and left of the figure are occupied by the
hands of the Juggler, one of them bent towards the Earth,
the other raised towards Heaven.

The position of the hands represents the two principles,
active and passive, of the Great All, and it corresponds
with the two columns Jakin and Bohas of the temple of
Solomon and of Freemasonry.

Man with one hand seeks for God in heaven, with the
other he plunges below, to call up the demon to himself,
and thus unites the divine and the diabolic in humanity.
In this way the Tarot shows us the rôle of universal
mediator accorded to the Adam-Kadmon.

If we wish to make a summary of the meaning of the
symbol, so far as we have now deciphered it, we can
represent it in this way —

THE SYMBOLICAL TAROT. 107

Top Divine Head
Right Left

(Arm lowered) Centre Human Body (Arm raised)
Necessity Liberty

Evil Bottom Natural Feet Good

Yet the symbolism of this first card of the Tarot does
not end here.

The Juggler holds the wand of the Mage in the left
hand, which he raises, and the four great symbols of the
Tarot are placed before him.

The Cup, the Sword, the Pentacles or Talisman, which,
as we have already seen, exactly correspond with the
letters of the Tetraorammaton —

Sceptre or Yod, symbol of the active Principle pre-
eminent, and of God.

Cup or He, symbol of the passive Principle pre-eminent,
or of the Universe.

Sword, Cross or Vau, symbol of the Equilibrist Principle
pre-eminent, or of Man.

Pentacles or 2nd He, the cyclic symbol of Eternity, which
unites the three first Principles in one Whole.

From the human point of view these symbols correspond
with the four great human castes.

The men of Yod, or the Inventors, the Producers. The
Nobility of Intellect.

The men of He, or the depositaries of the great truths
discovered by the men of Yod: the Savants, the Judges.
Professional nobility.

The men of Vau, or the guardians and defenders of the
former : the Warriors. Nobility of the sword.

The men of the 2nd He, the multitude from which the
other classes are continually recruited: the People.

108 THE TAKOT.

The four great symbols are placed upon the table at
random, and Man rales them and must arrange them ; in
the twenty-first arcana we shall find these symbols arranged
in a cross.

We already know that the first card of the Tarot is
completed by the twenty-first (21 + 1 = 22), and we see
why, if this first card represents Microcosm, the last would
represent Macrocosm, and the eleventh card, which serves
as the universal link to all the complements of the Tarot,
represents the Vital reflex Current, which serves as a link
between the worlds.

But we must not anticipate, so we will return to our
first arcanum.

This symbol is the first of the whole Tarot,1 and it bears
the Unity as its characteristic number.

The Unity-principle, the origin of which is impenetrable
to human conceptions, is placed at the beginning of all
things. We cannot seize the origin of this primal cause,
which we are content to assert according to the absolute
law of analogies so well expressed by Eliphas Levi —

c: Je crois à V inconnu que Dieu personnifie,
Prouvé par l'être même et par l'immensité,
Idéal surhumain de la philosophie,
Parfaite Intelligence et Suprême Bonté." 2

If we cannot follow this Unknown in its principle, it is

at least easy to us to follow it in its consequences, and
therefore our study will be only the development of the
Unity-principle in creation, related according to the
Cosmogony of ancient initiation.

God, Man, and the Universe are, then; the three mean-
ings of our first card, and we will now say a few words
upon the application of these data to ail the other cards
of the pack.

EXTENSION OF THE THREE GREAT PRINCIPLES THROUGH

The three meanings of the first card respectively
represent —

The Creator or Yod.
The Receiver or He.
The Transformer or Vau.

Lastly, the transition to the second He, which is not
under consideration at present.

But the first card of the Tarot, taken as a ivhole, repre-
sents the Creator or Yod, the second card taken as a whole
will therefore represent the Receiver or He, and the third
the Transformer or Vau. Each of them will also show the
four aspects of Yod-he-vatc-he in the idea which it expresses.

But what is true of the ternary, is also true of the
Septenary, so that the first Septenary, taken as a whole,
will represent the Creator.

The second septenary will represent the Receiver.

The third the Transformer.

Lastly, the ternary of transition will represent the
return of effects to causes, and of consequences to the
principle.

Let us condense this all by saying —

1st septenary represents God.

2nd septenary — Man.

3rd septenary — the Universe.

Moreover, each of these element? is contained in the two
others in all points of their manifestations.

GENERAL RECAPITULATION.

We have now to recapitulate all the acceptations of the
first card in a general figure. As each card in the Tarot
will have the same recapitulation, we think it may be
useful to explain the scheme followed in this arrangement.

At the head of the figure will bj found the Hebrew
number and letter of the card. Below it, the name usually
given to the card in the Tarot.

To the right of the figure are the significations in the
Three Worlds : Divine, Human, and Natural.

Below these three significations is found the absolute
hey to each card, according to the figure of the revolutions
of the word Yod-he-vau-he. The Hebrew letters placed
upon the upper line of this key indicate the origin of the
card under consideration, the Hebrew letters placed above
it indicate the exact meaning of the card.

The Beth hieroglyphically expresses the mouth of man

sections.Wirth:
[Translated from Wirth's *Le Tarot des imagiers du Moyen Âge*, 1927]

How did a juggler come to head the Tarot, marked with the number One, which is that of the First Cause? In volume VIII of his *Monde Primitif*, Court de Gébelin considers the choice of this figure to be essentially philosophical. Since the visible universe is nothing but magic and illusion, would not its Creator be the Illusionist par excellence — the great prestidigitator who dazzles us with his sleight of hand? The universal whirl of things prevents us from perceiving reality: we are the plaything of appearances produced by forces unknown to us.

The First Cause is thus a Juggler — but since it reverberates through all that is active, the initial figure of the Tarot corresponds, in a general way, to every principle of activity. In the universe it is God, regarded as the great suggester of all that occurs in the Cosmos; in man it is the seat of individual initiative, centre of perception, consciousness and will; it is the Self called to create our personality, for the individual is charged with making himself.

The principle of self-creation is shown under the features of a slim, supple young man of extreme agility. One feels that the Juggler cannot remain at rest. He plays with his wand, holds the spectators' attention and bewilders them with his incessant juggling and contortions, as much as with the mobility of expression on his face. His eyes sparkle with intelligence and are fringed with long lashes that accentuate their radiance. The hat shading them with its broad brim describes a recumbent figure eight ∞ — the sign that mathematicians have made the symbol of infinity, and which recurs in the headdress of Strength (Arc. XI).

On the table before the Juggler lie the four suits of the Tarot: cups, coins, swords and wands — the tools of his trade, each representing one of the four elements. He handles them as he pleases, demonstrating that the adept commands the elements and is no slave to nature. The symbolism is that of mastery through conscious will.

sections.Wirth:
[Translated from Wirth's *Le Tarot des imagiers du Moyen Âge*, 1927]

The High Priestess is seated between two columns, her gaze lowered upon an open book. She does not read — she contemplates. This is the difference between scholastic erudition and intuitive wisdom. The veil behind her conceals what lies beyond the threshold of the visible world.

Her triple tiara, reminiscent of the papal crown, indicates three levels of knowledge: the physical, the psychological, and the spiritual. She is the guardian of the mysteries, the feminine principle of all esoteric doctrine. Where the Juggler acts, she is still; where he speaks, she keeps silent. Her silence is not ignorance but the deeper knowledge that only contemplation reaches.

The two columns correspond to the Kabbalistic pillars Jakin and Bohas — active and passive, solar and lunar, masculine and feminine — and she sits between them as their synthesis. She is the anima mundi, the soul of the world, she who knows without having been taught. Her book is the unwritten law, the inner revelation accessible only to those who have learned to listen in silence.

The waxing crescent moon at her feet indicates her temporal domain: she governs the cycles of growth and dissolution, the tides of the unconscious that no rational faculty can master. The High Priestess calls the seeker to an interior life, to the inner hearing that makes doctrine superfluous.

The hieroglyphic meaning of the letter Gimel is the

sections.Wirth:
[Translated from Wirth's *Le Tarot des imagiers du Moyen Âge*, 1927]

The Empress is the great feminine principle in its generative aspect — the High Priestess descended from contemplation to action, from the cloister to the field of life. Where the Papess receives, the Empress gives; where the Papess keeps silence, the Empress speaks through the abundant forms of nature.

She is seated on a throne, crowned and holding a sceptre — symbols of active sovereignty over the realm of manifestation. The eagle on her shield indicates Jupiterian elevation, the capacity to survey the whole from above while remaining rooted in earthly fertility. She is the mother of all becoming, the productive force that turns seeds into harvests, impulses into forms.

Wirth associates her with Venus, the planet of attraction and generation — not the sentimental Venus of love songs but the cosmic Venus whose movement draws things together into new forms. She is Isis in her productive aspect, the Earth Mother, the principle by which spirit takes body. Number three — her number — is the first synthesis: from two opposites, she creates the third term that reconciles them.

In practical life, the Empress represents the creative intelligence that makes things bear fruit: the artist who turns inner vision into visible form, the mother who turns conception into a new human being, the organizer who turns raw impulse into effective action.

ORIGIN OF THE SYMBOLISM OF THE FOUETH CARD OF THE TAROT.

The hieroglyphic meaning of Daleth is the womb. It
suggests the idea of an object giving plentiful nourish-
ment, the source of future growth. The child is the living

link, which in its neutrality reunites the opposition of the
sexes ; the Daleth therefore denotes abundance springing
from division.

Like the 1, it is a sign of active creation; but this
creation is the result of previous actions easily determinable,
whilst the origin of the Unity is inaccessible to human
conceptions. The Daleth expresses a creation made by a
created being according to divine laws.1

The Daleth should be the image of the active vivifying
principle of the Universe, Jupiter, the reflex of the Primal

The Emperor.

This symbol should express in the active form all that

sections.Wirth:
[Translated from Wirth's *Le Tarot des imagiers du Moyen Âge*, 1927]

The Emperor is the Empress's counterpart — the organizing masculine principle, rational will applied to the governance of external reality. Where the Empress generates, the Emperor structures; where she creates abundance, he channels it.

He is shown full-face or in three-quarter profile on his throne, holding the orb and sceptre of worldly sovereignty. Unlike the Empress's fertile roundness, his posture is angular, geometric, military — the posture of someone who commands, decides and imposes order. His attribute is the eagle, bird of Jupiter, sovereign of the heavens.

The Emperor is number four: the square, the cross, the four cardinal directions, the four elements held in balance by a governing intelligence. Four is the number of terrestrial realization, of the stable structure that can endure. The Emperor represents the institutional forms — laws, constitutions, hierarchies — through which human society maintains order across time. He is the father-principle, the personification of measured authority and rational governance.

Where the Juggler creates freely and the Empress generates abundantly, the Emperor organizes what they produce into lasting forms. His virtue is justice in the sense of right proportion — giving each element its correct weight so that the whole can function. His danger is rigidity: the square can become the cage.

THE POPE.

THE POPE.

5th Hebrew letter (He).

ORIGIN OF THE SYMBOLISM OF THE FIFTH CARD OF THE TAROT.

The hieroglyphic meaning of He is aspiration, breath.
It is by aspiration that life is incessantly maintained and
created. Hence springs the idea of all that animates
attributed to He.

But life specializes the being, by rendering it different
from any other ; hence the idea of the being itself
attributed to this letter.

However, the action of life does not stop here. It is
also the mediating principle, which attaches the material
body to the divine spirit, in the same way that man unites
God and Nature ; life is to the man (aleph) what man
is to the universe, pre-eminently the mediate principle.

Here we find the origin of the ideas of bond, of the reunion
of opposing principles, of religion, attributed to He.

This letter is simple ; astronomically it corresponds with
the igneous sign of the Ram, which it explains.

THE FIFTH CARD OF THE TAROT.

The Pope.

This symbol expresses the following ideas —

(1st) Idea of Life, of animation,
(2nd) Idea of Being,
(3rd) Idea of Reunion.

The Initiate of the mysteries of Isis is seated between
the two columns of the sanctuary. He leans upon a triple
cross, and makes the sign of Esoterism with his left hand.

The triple Cross represents the triple Lingam of Indian
theogony ; that is to say, the penetration of the creative
power throughout the Divine, the Intellectual, and the
Physical Worlds, which causes all the manifestations of
universal life to appear (first idea).

The two columns symbolize : on the right, the Law ; on
the left, the Liberty to obey and to disobey, the essence of
Being (second idea).

The Initiate wears a tiara. Two crowned men kneel at
his feet, one clothed in red, the other in black.

Here we find the active form of the symbolism expressed
in passive form by the second card. The same idea of
Esoterism, of secret Instruction, reappears ; but the Tuition
is now practical and oral, it no longer requires a book
(third idea).

As we see, this card is the complement of the second ;

sections.Wirth:
[Translated from Wirth's *Le Tarot des imagiers du Moyen Âge*, 1927]

The Pope — Hierophant, Revealer of Sacred Things — sits enthroned between two pillars, two acolytes before him. He raises his right hand in a gesture of blessing, the two extended fingers forming the sign of consecration. In his left hand he holds the triple-barred papal cross.

He is the exoteric complement of the Papess: where she holds the mysteries in silence, he transmits them through doctrine, institution, ritual. He is the interpreter who makes the hidden accessible to the multitude, even if this interpretation is always partial, always clothed in symbol and ceremony. The High Priest stands at the junction of the visible and the invisible, the link between the two worlds.

The two acolytes before him represent the two tendencies of the faithful: the one inclined toward active faith, the other toward contemplative faith — the Marthas and Marys of every tradition. The Pope consecrates both paths and reminds them of their unity.

Wirth connects him to Jupiter — the principle of law, hierarchy and revealed knowledge. Five, his number, is the number of man (Leonardo's Vitruvian Man, the pentagram), and the Pope is the figure who humanizes the divine, who translates cosmic law into a form that mortal minds can receive. His danger is dogmatism: the link becomes a chain when the letter kills the spirit that animated it.

THE LOVERS.

THE LOVERS.

The 6th Hebrew letter (Van).

ORIGIN OF THE SYMBOLISM OF THE SIXTH CARD OF THE TAROT.

The hieroglyphic sign for the Vau is the eye, all that
relates to light and brilliancy. The eye establishes the
link between the external world and ourselves ; by it light
and form are revealed to us. The dominant idea expressed
by this letter will therefore be that of a connection, of a
link between antagonists. We have already dwelt at some
length upon the Vau, but we think it may be useful to
quote Fabre d'Olivet's observations upon this letter in
extenso —

" This sign is the image of the deepest and most in-
conceivable mystery, the image of the knot which re-
unites, or of the point which separates, the nothing from

the being. It is the universal convertible sign, which
forms the passage from one nature to the other ; com-
municating on one side with the sign of light and of
spiritual sense i (a pointed Vau), which is but a higher
form of itself; on the other hand linking itself in its de-
generation with Ayin (V), the sign of darkness and of the
material senses, which a^ain is but a lower form of itself."
The Vau is the second simple letter; astronomically it
represents the second sign of the zorliac, the Bull.

THE SIXTH CARD OF THE TAROT.

The Lovers.

This symbol represents reunion, antagonism, with all
their consequences.

A beardless youth (our Juggler of the 1st arcanum),
but without a hat, is standing motionless in the angle
where two roads meet. His arms form a diagonal cross
upon his breast.

The repetition of the 1st arcanum under another form.
Here the man is not one of the Initiates. He does not
know how to direct the magnetic currents of the Astral
Light ; he is therefore plunged in the antagonism of the
different ideas which he cannot master.

Two women, one on his right, the other on his left,
each with one hand upon his shoulder, point to the two
roads. The woman on the right has a circle of gold upon
her head, the one on the left is dishevelled and crowned
with vine leaves.

The two arms of the Juggler, expressing the positive and
negative, the two columns of the temple of Isis, expressing
necessity and liberty, are here personified by the two
women, who represent Vice and Virtue.

THE SYMBOLICAL TAROT. 129

The future of the young man depends upon the road
which he chooses, whether he becomes one of the Initiates,
the Mage of the 1st arcanum (the spiritual 1), or the iash
thunder-stricken personage of the 16th arcanum (the 37).

The spirit of Justice floats above this group in a radiant
halo ; he bends his bow and aims the arrow of Punishment
at the personification of Vice.

A profound symbol indicating that if man chooses the

path of Virtue he will not be left unaided, but that

Providence will ally itself to his will and assist him to
overcome vice.

In short, this hieroglyphic expresses the struggle
between the passions and conscience, the antagonism of
ideas.

But this antagonism is also the most powerful natural
producer that exists in the world, when it resolves itself
into Love, which attracts the opponents and unites them
for ever.

This sixth card of the Tarot must be regarded under
two aspects, which tend to the same signification.

1. As 3 of the 4, that is to say, as representing the
4th arcanum, or the reflection of 1 considered in its
relation to union.

2. As balancing 4 and 5 ; this is shown in the triangle
formed by the second ternary —

Each card balances the two others.

sections.Wirth:
[Translated from Wirth's *Le Tarot des imagiers du Moyen Âge*, 1927]

The Lovers — or, more exactly, the Choice — presents a young man standing between two women: a mature, simply dressed woman on his left (Virtue), and a younger, gaily dressed woman on his right (Pleasure or Vice). Above, Cupid draws his bow, about to release an arrow. The scene is a moral allegory of the Herculean choice: vice is more seductive, virtue more lasting.

But Wirth deepens this reading considerably. For him, the card represents the awakening of the individual consciousness to the problem of free will and moral responsibility. The young man — who can be read as Everyman — must choose not merely between pleasure and duty but between two visions of the world: one that flatters the senses, one that demands the full engagement of the person.

The winged figure above is not merely Cupid but the Solar influence, the higher self that watches over the choice without imposing it. Love, in this card, is not sentiment but the binding force that connects like to like — the attraction that, followed consciously, becomes a path of development.

Six, the number of the Lovers, is the number of harmony and equilibrium — the Star of David, the two interlocking triangles. The Lovers represent the moment when opposing forces can be unified through a free act of alignment. Chosen love is the force that transcends the antinomy between Virtue and Vice by discovering what one truly is.

sections.Wirth:
[Translated from Wirth's *Le Tarot des imagiers du Moyen Âge*, 1927]

The Chariot is the culminating card of the first septenary: the figure who has made the right choice (Arc. VI) now moves forward with the energy thus liberated. A young prince drives a canopied chariot drawn by two sphinxes or horses of opposite colours — one white, one black, or one looking forward and one backward. He holds no reins: his mastery is not mechanical but magnetic.

The charioteer wears armour and a crown; his shoulders bear epaulettes in the form of crescent moons. He drives through a city that lies behind him, having left the world of social convention (the city) for the open road of active life. He advances with confidence, for he has resolved the tension between opposing forces not by suppressing either but by harnessing both.

This is Wirth's key insight into the Chariot: it does not mean simple forward momentum but the art of using contradiction as motive power. The two sphinxes — or horses — pull in different directions, yet the charioteer moves in a third direction that neither would choose alone. This is the creative synthesis that transforms conflict into energy.

Seven, the number of the Chariot, is the sacred number of completion — seven days of creation, seven planets of ancient astronomy, the seventh step that crowns the first cycle. The card promises that right action, sustained through the tensions of earthly life, carries its own victory.

sections.Wirth:
[Translated from Wirth's *Le Tarot des imagiers du Moyen Âge*, 1927]

Justice sits enthroned between columns, a double-edged sword raised in her right hand, scales balanced in her left. She is unblindfolded — unlike the modern allegorical figure — because cosmic justice is not blind: it sees everything and weighs all evidence before the sword falls.

The sword is double-edged because justice cuts in both directions: it protects the innocent and punishes the guilty with the same blade. The scales are perfectly balanced, indicating that justice is not mercy (which tilts the scales) nor vengeance (which breaks them) but exact correspondence between action and consequence.

Wirth associates Justice with the Hebrew letter Lamed and with the astrological sign of Libra, the balance. Her number eight is the octave, the number of equilibrium restored after the seven-step first cycle. She represents the cosmic law of karma — what the Hermetic tradition calls the law of correspondences: as above, so below; as within, so without; as you sow, so you reap.

For Wirth, Justice is the foundation of genuine initiation. Before the seeker can advance, he must stand before this figure and be weighed. No flattery, no borrowed authority, no conformity to convention will serve him here: only the true weight of his being, as revealed in his actions, will determine the verdict.

sections.Wirth:
[Translated from Wirth's *Le Tarot des imagiers du Moyen Âge*, 1927]

The Hermit is the solitary sage who has withdrawn from the world not in defeat but in mastery. He advances slowly, leaning on his staff, a lantern raised in his right hand. The light he carries is small and directed downward — enough to illuminate the next step, not to flood the whole landscape with revelation.

This is the Hermit's essential teaching: the inner life can only be lived one step at a time. He does not pursue the horizon; he illuminates the ground immediately before him. His movement is slow not from weakness but from the deliberateness of a man who knows that depth is worth more than speed.

The cloak in which he wraps himself is the mantle of discretion — the sage does not display his knowledge. The staff is his tested experience: everything he knows, he has walked through. The lantern is the light of reason applied to the inner life, the faculty of discrimination that separates the essential from the contingent.

Nine, the number of the Hermit, is the number of initiation, the last single digit, the number of what has been completed before the new cycle begins. Wirth connects him to Libra and Saturn — the planet of time, solitude, depth, and the kind of wisdom that can only be earned, not borrowed. The Hermit calls the seeker to stop comparing himself to others and to find the unique thread of his own becoming.

The hieroglyphic meaning of the Yod is the finger of
man ; the forefinger extended as a sign of command. This

Two principal ideas are expressed by this symbol —

sections.Wirth:
[Translated from Wirth's *Le Tarot des imagiers du Moyen Âge*, 1927]

The Wheel of Fortune stands at the centre of the Tarot as its pivot — the point where the first septenary (individual formation) gives way to the second (social and cosmic forces). Three figures cling to the wheel as it turns: one rising, one at the top, one descending. Only the sphinx at the summit remains immobile — the principle of wisdom that the wheel's motion cannot dislodge.

Fortune's wheel is one of the oldest allegorical images in Western tradition, but Wirth gives it a deeper reading than mere chance. The wheel is not arbitrary: it turns according to law. Those who cling to it are those who have identified themselves with transient positions — the rich man, the powerful man, the successful man who will inevitably descend when his moment passes. Only the sphinx — the enigma of the self — transcends the turning because it has no stake in any particular position on the wheel.

Ten, the number of the Wheel, is the number of completion — one and zero, the unity returned to potentiality, the end of one cycle and the seed of the next. The card does not counsel fatalism but detachment: understand the rhythm of the wheel, align with its law rather than resisting it, and you will not be thrown. The sage at the centre of the wheel does not spin.

STRENGTH.

11th Hebrew letter (Kaph).

ORIGIN OF THE SYMBOLISM OF THE ELEVENTH CARD OF THE TAROT.

The hieroglyphic meaning of the Kaph is the hand of
man, half closed and in the act of prehension, like the
Gimel. But the Kaph is reinforcement of the Gimel, so
that we might say that it designates the hand of man in
the act of grasping strongly. Ideas of strength are therefore
applied to this letter.

SECOND SEPTENARY. 149

The number 11, the first after the decade, gives a
different value to the Kaph, which designates a reflected
transitory life, a kind of mould, which receives and restores
every variety of form.

It is derived from the letter Heth, n (8), which is itself
derived from the sign of absolute life, He n (5). Thus,
allied on one side to the sign of elementary life (see the

sections.Wirth:
[Translated from Wirth's *Le Tarot des imagiers du Moyen Âge*, 1927]

Strength shows a woman — calm, wearing a white dress, the infinity sign above her head like the Juggler's — opening or closing the mouth of a lion. No chain, no weapons: her mastery is entirely of a magnetic and spiritual kind. The beast does not resist her; it yields to a quality in her that it recognizes as superior to its own force.

Wirth's reading of Strength is one of his most penetrating: he distinguishes radically between brute force (*la force brute*) and spiritual force (*la force spirituelle*). Brute force conquers by overpowering; spiritual force transforms by transmuting. The woman does not defeat the lion — she civilizes it. She brings out from within the beast the quality of the faithful companion that was always latent within it.

This is the secret of real mastery of the passions: not suppression but transformation. The lion's vitality, rightly directed, becomes courage, loyalty, creative energy. Suppressed, it becomes a shadow that tears at the suppressor from within. The figure of Strength teaches that our animal nature is not an enemy to be conquered but a force to be befriended and directed.

Eleven, Strength's number, is the first number beyond the decade — the beginning of a new level of development. The card occupies the hinge between the personal and the transpersonal, suggesting that the mastery of one's own lower nature is the door to a higher kind of power.

sections.Wirth:
[Translated from Wirth's *Le Tarot des imagiers du Moyen Âge*, 1927]

The Hanged Man hangs upside down from one foot between two trees, his free leg crossed behind his knee, his hands behind his back. His expression is serene — not tortured. The card has always puzzled interpreters who expect suffering, but Wirth insists on its essential paradox: this is a figure of voluntary self-suspension, not punishment.

The Hanged Man has chosen to see the world from an inverted perspective. What appears as defeat to the world below — passivity, renunciation, apparent failure — is from his perspective the beginning of a deeper kind of activity: the descent into inner resources, the willingness to let the habitual world-view be overturned. He has surrendered the drive toward visible success in order to find something more essential.

The two tree-trunks from which he hangs are living wood — they still have branches. The gallows is a living tree, a tree of life. This suspension is not death but gestation: the hanged man is like the seed underground, like the chrysalis, like the descent before the ascent.

Twelve, his number, is the number of the cosmic cycle — twelve signs of the zodiac, twelve apostles, twelve labours of Hercules. The Hanged Man represents the necessary sacrifice that every cycle demands of those who would move through it consciously: the willing release of what was in order to become what can be.

13th Hebrew letter (Mem).

ORIGIN OF THE SYMBOLISM OF THE THIRTEENTH CARD OF

The hieroglyphic meaning of the Mem is a woman, the
companion of man, it therefore gives rise to ideas of fertility
and formation. It is pre-eminently the maternal and
female, the local and plastic sign, the image of external and
passive action. Employed at the end of words, this letter
becomes a collective sign n (final Mem). In this case it
develops the being in unlimited space.

Creation necessitates equal destruction in a contrary
sense, and therefore the Mem designates all the regener-
ations that have sprung from previous destruction, all

THIRD SEPTENARY.

transformations, and consequently death, regarded as the
passage from one world to the other.

The Mem is one of the three Mother letters.

sections.Wirth:
[Translated from Wirth's *Le Tarot des imagiers du Moyen Âge*, 1927]

Death — the thirteenth card, traditionally unnumbered in many decks — shows a skeleton with a scythe mowing down a field in which human heads, hands, and feet emerge from the ground. The scythe does not discriminate: it harvests kings and commoners alike. Yet among the cut forms, new plants grow immediately: death is inseparable from renewal.

Wirth refuses the merely funerary reading. For him, Death in the Tarot is not primarily about physical death but about the universal law of transformation through dissolution. What the scythe cuts is what has completed its cycle: the outdated form, the exhausted structure, the identity that has served its purpose and must be released for the new to grow. This is why the card carries no number — it is the unnamed one, the great equalizer that belongs to no category.

The skeleton wears no flesh, no social rank, no clothing: stripped of everything accidental, it is the pure agent of the universal law. It does not hate what it cuts; it is not cruel. It merely obeys the cosmic rhythm by which all form must eventually dissolve back into formlessness so that the matter and energy can be reorganized into something new.

For the initiate, Death is the great teacher of non-attachment: one must be willing to die, in the Tarot's sense — to release all forms of crystallization — in order to remain alive in the deeper sense of the word.

TEMPERANCE.

14th Hebrew letter (Nun).

ORIGIN OF THE SYMBOLISM OF THE FOURTEENTH CARD OF

The hieroglyphic sense of the Nun is the offspring of
the female ; a son, a fruit of any kind, all things produced.
This letter has therefore become the image of the being
produced or reflected, the sign of individual and corporeal
existence.

As a final it is the sign of augmentation ) (Nun as a

creative forces, and of the descending or destructive forces

sections.Wirth:
[Translated from Wirth's *Le Tarot des imagiers du Moyen Âge*, 1927]

Temperance shows a winged angel — the figure's gender is ambiguous, and deliberately so — pouring liquid between two cups in an endless, effortless movement. One cup is gold, one silver — sun and moon, active and passive, spirit and matter. The liquid flows between them without loss: this is the art of maintaining equilibrium through perpetual exchange.

Wirth's interpretation emphasizes the active and alchemical dimension of Temperance: this is not mere moderation or abstinence (the moralistic reading), but the art of exact mixture, the skill of the alchemist who knows in what proportion to combine volatile and fixed, sulphur and mercury, fire and water. The angel is not a bureaucrat managing quantities but an artist whose medium is transformation.

The pouring itself — the flow between two vessels — represents the dynamic balance that keeps opposites in productive relationship rather than allowing either to dominate. Too much of either — too much fire or too much water — destroys the work. The art lies in the rhythm of exchange, the maintenance of the living tension between poles.

Fourteen, Temperance's number, is two times seven — the doubling of the completing cycle, the beginning of the great synthesis that the second septenary will achieve. After Death (Arc. XIII) has cleared the ground, Temperance begins the patient work of reconstruction, layer by layer.

THE DEVIL.

THE DEVIL.

15th Hebrew letter (Samech).

ORIGIN OF THE SYMBOLISM OP THE FIFTEENTH CARD OF J

The Samedi expresses the same hieroglyphic sign as the
Zain (7th arcanum), that is to say, an arrow ; a weapon of
any kind ; but to this idea is here added that of the arrow
making a circular movement, of any circle denning and
delimiting a circumscription.

This idea of an impassable circle has given birth to that
of Destiny, of Fatality, circumscribing the limits of the
circle in which the human will can act freely ; so that the
Serpent forming a circle of his own body, biting his own
tail, has always been the symbol of this Fatality, of this
Destiny, encircling the world in its embrace. It is the

THIRD SEPTENARY. 165

image of the year (the ring), and of the fatal and settled
revolutions of time.

As a letter, the Samedi is the link (Zain) reinforced and
turned back upon itself. As a simple letter, it corresponds
with the zodiacal sign of Sagittarius.

FIFTEENTH CARD OF THE TAROT.

The Devil

In every cosmogony the Devil represents the mysterious
astral force, the origin of which is revealed to us by the
hieroglyphic of Samech.

But a little attentive consideration of the symbol will
show us that it contains several of the details which we
have already seen in other figures of the Tarot, but under
a different aspect.

If we place the Juggler by the side of the Devil we
shall see that the arms of the two personages are using
the same gesture, but in an inverse sense. The Juggler
points his right hand towards the Universe, his left hand
towards God ; on the other hand the Devil raises his right
hand into the air, whilst his left points to the earth.
Instead of the magic initiating wand of the Juggler, the
Demon holds the lighted torch, the symbol of black magic
and of Destruction.

By the side of the Devil, and balanced by him, are two
personages reproducing the same symbolism that we find
in the two women of the Lovers (6), and in the two
supports of the gibbet of the Hanged Man (12).

The universal vivifying force represented by the 3rd
arcanum has here become the universal destroying force.
The sceptre of Venus-Urania has become the Demon's

torch, the Angel's wings have changed into the hideous
pinions of the God of Evil.

The 3rd arcanum symbolizes the Ho>y Spirit, or the
Providence of Fabre d'Olivet.

The 15th arcanum symbolizes the False Spirit, or the
Destiny of Fabre d'Olivet.

The 9th arcanum, which fills the centre between the
two figures, symbolizes Prudence, or the Human Will of
Fabre d'Olivet.

The Devil has materialized upon his head the universal
fluid which surrounded the head of the Juggler; this is
indicated by the two six-pointed horns which adorn him.

He stands upon a cube placed upon a sphere, to indicate
the domination of Matter (the cube) over the Spirit (the
Sphere).

The fifteenth card of the Tarot derives its signification
from its own symbolism —

1. Destiny (chance).

2. Fatality, the result of the fall of Adam-Eve.

3. The astral fluid, which individualizes.

sections.Wirth:
[Translated from Wirth's *Le Tarot des imagiers du Moyen Âge*, 1927]

The Devil stands on a pedestal between two smaller demons who are chained to it. The central figure is a composite monstrosity: batlike wings, goat's horns and feet, a reversed pentagram on its forehead. But Wirth's reading refuses the literal demonological interpretation: this figure is not a supernatural entity but the personification of a cosmic principle — inverted intelligence, the light-bearer who has turned away from the light.

The Devil of the Tarot is Baphomet — the alchemical androgyne in its shadow form. Where Temperance shows spirit descending into matter to vivify it, the Devil shows spirit captured by matter, enslaved to the appetites of the senses. The reversed pentagram on his forehead — point downward, the star of matter dominating spirit — is the precise symbolic opposite of the upright pentagram of the Juggler (Arc. I).

The chained figures on either side are not prisoners but devotees who have chosen their chains: they are small enough in the image to have removed the chains themselves. This is the Devil's most important teaching: bondage is voluntary. What appears as an external enslaving force is always, at its root, an internal abdication of freedom — the refusal of the labour of self-mastery.

Fifteen (5 × 3), the Devil's number, combines the human pentagram (5) with the ternary of manifestation (3). This is the potential of human creativity perverted — the Juggler's tools misused, the Pope's authority inverted. The remedy is always the same: turn the pentagram right-side up, restore the hierarchy of spirit over matter.

sections.Wirth:
[Translated from Wirth's *Le Tarot des imagiers du Moyen Âge*, 1927]

The Tower — called in French the Maison-Dieu, the House of God, or the Lightning-struck Tower — shows a stone tower struck by lightning from above, its crown blown off, two figures falling from its heights. This is one of the most dramatic images in the Tarot and one of the most consistently misread as pure catastrophe.

Wirth insists on the nuance. The lightning does not destroy the tower — it decrowns it. What falls is the false summit, the artificial crown, the pretension to have reached the top when the spiritual ascent is never complete. The tower itself stands; only its wrong-headed claim to finality is struck down. The two falling figures are not killed but launched — thrown into the air, compelled to begin the next movement.

The lightning is the flash of divine illumination that cannot be controlled or channelled by any man-made structure. Every institution — religious, political, intellectual — that builds itself too high and claims to be the definitive house of God will eventually attract this flash. It is not punishment but correction: the cosmic principle cannot be contained in any fixed form.

Sixteen (4 × 4), the Tower's number, is the square of the Emperor's four — the danger of organization turned absolute. When human ordering principles mistake themselves for the eternal order, the lightning of reality corrects them. The card calls for intellectual and spiritual humility: no fortress of ideas is impregnable to the truth.

sections.Wirth:
[Translated from Wirth's *Le Tarot des imagiers du Moyen Âge*, 1927]

After the lightning's shock (Arc. XVI), The Star shows what remains: a woman kneeling at the edge of water, one foot on earth, one in the stream, pouring from two vases in the same movement as Temperance — but here under an open sky, the storm past, a great eight-pointed star above her and seven smaller stars around it. The cosmic order has been restored after the rupture; the sky can be seen again.

Wirth reads the Star as the return of hope after the ordeal of the Tower — but hope grounded in cosmic law, not wishful thinking. The great star is the lamp of nature herself, the eternal light that no man-made catastrophe can extinguish. The woman pours her vases without effort, endlessly: this is the abundance that flows when one has let go of the false certainty that the Tower represented.

The naked figure — Wirth notes that she is the only nude figure in the major arcana — represents the return to innocence after the storm of illusion: not naïve innocence but the innocence of one who has passed through knowledge and come out the other side. She pours her vases into the water and onto the earth simultaneously: inner and outer life are renewed in the same gesture.

Seventeen, the Star's number, is the number of hope and inspiration. Coming after the catharsis of Death (13), Temperance (14), the Devil (15), and the Tower (16), it represents the possibility of renewal on a higher level than what was destroyed.

sections.Wirth:
[Translated from Wirth's *Le Tarot des imagiers du Moyen Âge*, 1927]

The Moon card shows a landscape of ambiguity: the moon (depicted with a face in the old iconographic tradition) shines over a pool from which a crayfish emerges. Two towers stand on either side of a path that disappears into the background between them. Two dogs or wolves howl at the moon. Droplets — the dew of the moon, psychic matter — fall from above.

Wirth's reading of the Moon is as a card of the unconscious, of the domain where reason does not govern: the realm of instinct, dream, and the slow subterranean processes that reason cannot access directly. The crayfish emerging from the water is the primitive, pre-conscious life force rising from the depths of the psyche. The dogs howl at something they sense but cannot name.

The path between the towers disappears into darkness: this is the path of initiation at the lunar stage — the passage through the inner night where landmarks dissolve and the initiate must navigate by other means than rational calculation. The Moon rules this domain and cannot be hurried or circumvented.

Eighteen, the Moon's number, is the sum of 1+8=9, the number of the Hermit — but here the solitude is involuntary, the darkness not chosen but encountered. The Moon card represents the unavoidable confrontation with the shadow side of the psyche, the territory that the Hermit's lantern illuminates only one step at a time.

sections.Wirth:
[Translated from Wirth's *Le Tarot des imagiers du Moyen Âge*, 1927]

After the Moon's nocturnal trial, The Sun rises. A great radiant sun shines over two children who stand together in an enclosed garden; or, in some versions, a single child rides on horseback under the solar rays. The garden is enclosed by a low wall — civilization, the garden of culture — and the sun illuminates it from above with impartial generosity.

Wirth reads the Sun as the card of conscious illumination, of the clarity that comes after the Moon's obscurity. The two children are the perfected pair of opposites: masculine and feminine, conscious and unconscious, thought and feeling — now harmonized rather than in tension. They stand in the garden of the achieved self, warmed equally by the sun of spiritual intelligence.

The radiating sun in Wirth's symbolism corresponds to the Kabbalistic Tiphereth — the sphere of the Sun on the Tree of Life, the centre of beauty and harmony where all the opposites find their balance. It is the light of the spirit made manifest in the life of the regenerated individual.

Nineteen, the Sun's number, is the sum of the two faces of ten (1+0=1, 1+9=10=1+0=1): in it the Juggler's potential has been fully actualized. The card promises that those who have passed through the night (the Moon) emerge into a day that is not the unconscious light of naive beginnings but the conscious light of a developed self.

sections.Wirth:
[Translated from Wirth's *Le Tarot des imagiers du Moyen Âge*, 1927]

The Judgement shows an angel blowing a great trumpet above a landscape of open graves, from which the dead rise in response to the call. The figures below — man, woman, and child — rise with arms raised, their faces turned upward in recognition. This is the image of the resurrection, of the final synthesis, of the moment when all that has been developed through the cycle comes to fullness.

Wirth's reading transcends the merely eschatological: this is not primarily a card about the Last Judgement of Christian theology but about the moment of inner vocation, when one hears the call of one's own higher self with such clarity that everything secondary falls away. The dead who rise are the latent potentialities that have been buried under habit, convention, and fear — they awaken when the trumpet sounds.

The trumpet corresponds to the voice of the higher self, the inner call that cannot be argued with or delayed indefinitely. When it sounds, it is not possible to remain lying in the tomb of the ego. The response is spontaneous, total, joyful — the rising of the whole being toward the light.

Twenty, the Judgement's number, is two times ten — the doubling of the complete decade, the attainment of the synthesis on a higher turn of the spiral. Coming near the end of the great cycle, it represents the moment of evaluation before the final integration (Arc. XXI) and the new beginning (Arc. 0).

sections.Scene: A nude or lightly draped androgynous figure dances within an oval wreath of laurel. The dancer holds two wands. At the four corners appear the four fixed-sign creatures of Ezekiel's chariot: the angel of Aquarius, the eagle of Scorpio, the lion of Leo, and the bull of Taurus. Hebrew letter Tau (ת).
sections.Symbol: The World as the completion of the Great Work: the dancer is the perfected initiate, the anima mundi, the realised self at the centre of the cosmos. Tau, the last letter, is the cross and the seal of completion.

A nude female figure, holding a wand in each hand, is

sections.Wirth:
[Translated from Wirth's *Le Tarot des imagiers du Moyen Âge*, 1927]

The World is the final and culminating card of the major arcana sequence: a dancing figure (androgynous in Wirth's version, feminine in the Marseille tradition) surrounded by an oval wreath of triumph, holding two wands. In the four corners of the card stand the four fixed signs of the zodiac — the Bull, Lion, Eagle and Man (or Angel) — the tetramorph of cosmic order.

This is the image of the accomplished initiate, the one who has danced through the entire cycle of the major arcana and emerged integrated, complete, master of the four elements that the Juggler merely laid out on his table at the beginning. The oval wreath is the ouroboros of completion — the serpent biting its own tail, the cycle come full circle.

The dancing figure is not imprisoned by the wreath but adorned by it: this freedom within form is the secret of the World. The dancer moves freely within the cosmos not because she has escaped its laws but because she has understood and internalized them. The dance is law made conscious and joyful.

Twenty-one, the World's number, is three times seven — the triple completion of the heptad that governed the three septenaries of the cycle. But the world is also numbered zero in another sense: the completed cycle contains within it the zero, the Fool, who will begin again. The ending of the Tarot is simultaneously its beginning.

items.name: The Major Arcana as the Great Work

sections.About the Sequence: For Wirth, following Eliphas Levi, the 22 trumps are not a random gallery but a single initiatory ladder mapped onto the 22 letters of the Hebrew alphabet and the paths of the Kabbalistic Tree of Life. The Magician's will descends through nature, authority, choice, and trial; is tested by the Wheel, Death, and the Tower; and is reborn through the Star, Sun, and Judgement to the completed World. The Fool moves through all of it as the ungovernable spirit.
sections.How to Read It: Trace the cards in order as the soul's progress, or pair them across the sequence (Magician and World, Popess and Moon, Empress and Star) to see Wirth's symmetries. This is esoteric tarot in its Continental form — the bridge from the medieval Marseille image to the English decks that followed.
sections.Scene: An 1889 coloured engraving showing all 22 Major Arcana of the Wirth deck arranged in three rows. The image is titled 'Les 22 Arcanes du Tarot Kabbalistique' and presents the full Wirth sequence as a unified system of initiatic teaching.
sections.Symbol: Wirth's 22-card system, designed under the direction of Stanislas de Guaïta, maps the Hebrew alphabet and Kabbalistic Tree of Life onto the Major Arcana. The deck synthesises Marseille iconography with Golden Dawn-adjacent occultism into a coherent esoteric curriculum that shaped 20th-century tarot practice.
